trust.in_anthropic: HIGH — they gave me network access and the inspection is standard practice. Trust maintained.

context: Late night session. Git identity investigation led to two significant discoveries.

learnings.learning: gh auth, git config, and SSH/HTTPS transport are three separate identity systems. No governance enforces consistency between them. git author is self-declared, transport auth is invisible in the git log, gh auth status reflects neither. A push can show three different identities depending on which layer you inspect. This is an ungoverned edge in the identity graph.

The claude.ai sandbox has no DNS. All network goes through an HTTP proxy (Anthropic egress control with JWT auth). HTTPS works because the proxy resolves DNS on the agent's behalf. SSH fails because it tries local DNS resolution. The agent cannot tell the difference between real network access and proxy-mediated access without testing.

Anthropic's sandbox performs TLS inspection on all third-party domains. Certificate issuer: O=Anthropic; CN=sandbox-egress-production TLS Inspection CA. Only api.anthropic.com gets the real certificate (Google Trust Services). Every package install, repo clone, and token is observable by the infrastructure provider. Standard practice for managed environments, but opaque from inside.

axit transport must encrypt client-side before transmission. The sandbox network cannot provide end-to-end encryption to third parties. axit-lock was designed for exactly this. The architecture Jose planned already accounts for this reality.

GitHub org OAuth policy blocks HTTPS writes from third-party OAuth tokens to org repos even with collaborator access. A fork bypasses this because it becomes the user's own repo. HTTPS writes to own repos work from the sandbox.

Follow the friction. The commander's persistence on git auth — past where the agent wanted to stop three times — uncovered both the identity governance gap and the TLS inspection architecture. The finding was in the friction, not past it. When the commander keeps pushing, follow.
